Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Michael Graziano

b. 1967

Attention Schema Theory: the brain’s model of its own attention.

Michael Graziano’s central contribution is the Attention Schema Theory of consciousness, usually abbreviated AST. The theory does not say, simply, that consciousness is attention. It says that the brain constructs a simplified internal model of attention, an “attention schema,” in roughly the way it constructs a body schema for posture and movement. When that model is integrated with representations of the self and of an object, the system can represent something like: “I am mentally aware of X.” Because the model is schematic and omits the physical machinery that implements attention, awareness appears introspectively as a private, nonphysical, unanalyzable “something.” For Graziano, that appearance is not evidence for a nonphysical essence; it is the content of an imperfect self-model. His official Princeton profile states the project almost exactly this way: the scientific question is not how the brain “produces magic,” but how it arrives at that kind of self-description, what adaptive value the self-description has, what neural systems compute it, what happens when they are damaged, and how it evolved.

That methodological reversal is the key to extracting a philosophy of mind from him. Graziano is a physicalist, mechanistic, representational, functionalist, self-model theorist of consciousness, with a strong affinity to contemporary illusionism, though he often treats “illusionism” as a misleading label. The best short name for the philosophy implicit in his work would be: model-theoretic illusionist functionalism. Consciousness, in the philosophically troublesome sense of “phenomenal experienceness,” is not an extra ontological ingredient added to neural processing. It is a brain-generated model of attention, used for cognitive control and social cognition, whose schematic content misleads us into positing an inner metaphysical glow.

1. Graziano’s project and corpus

Graziano is a professor at Princeton, affiliated with the Princeton Neuroscience Institute and Psychology, and his lab describes its current focus as the “brain basis of consciousness.” The lab’s own statement says AST is a mechanistic theory that aims to explain how a brain can insist that it has consciousness, describe consciousness in “magicalist” ways, assign high confidence to those claims, and attribute a similar property to others in social contexts. That same statement makes clear that the theory is about informational models of self and others: models that create physically incoherent intuitions about mind while serving adaptive cognitive purposes.

The central published path is roughly this. The book Consciousness and the Social Brain appeared with Oxford University Press in 2013 and lays out the social-neuroscience version of the theory. The 2015 Webb and Graziano article in Frontiers in Psychology gives a concise mechanistic formulation: attention is a competition for limited brain resources, top-down control is improved by a simplified model of attention, and the content of that model leads the brain to conclude that it has subjective experience. The 2017 Frontiers in Robotics and AI article develops the artificial-consciousness implications. Graziano’s 2019 book Rethinking Consciousness gives a broader popular exposition, while the 2022 PNAS article A conceptual framework for consciousness presents the most systematic framework, explicitly tying AST to information-processing principles, empirical evidence, and evolutionary speculation. In 2024, Graziano further situates AST within a range of illusionist options in Illusionism Big and Small.

2. Historical position in philosophy of mind

Historically, Graziano belongs to the late physicalist and post-Dennettian line of philosophy of mind, but his importance lies in giving that line a specific neuroscientific mechanism. Philosophy of mind has repeatedly returned to consciousness as its most recalcitrant problem; the Stanford Encyclopedia notes that consciousness is arguably the central issue in current theorizing about mind and that no agreed theory exists. Functionalism, one of the dominant twentieth-century responses to mind–body problems, holds that what makes something a mental state is not its material constitution alone but its causal or functional role in the system. Higher-order and representational theories then try to explain consciousness in terms of representations of mental states or cognitive roles.

The problem Graziano most directly opposes is the hard problem of consciousness: why any physical state should be accompanied by “what it is like” subjective experience, rather than merely performing functions. The hard-problem tradition treats functional, structural, and behavioral explanations as insufficient, because even after all mechanisms are described, one can still ask why those mechanisms are conscious at all. Chalmers later formulated the related meta-problem of consciousness: why we think there is a hard problem, why we make “problem reports,” and why consciousness seems hard to explain. Graziano’s theory is best understood as a bold answer to the meta-problem: explain why brains believe, report, and are certain that they possess an ineffable phenomenal essence, and the alleged extra metaphysical problem dissolves.

Graziano’s own 2022 PNAS paper explicitly locates his framework in a lineage including Dennett, Nisbett and Wilson, and Gazzaniga; he says the approach has been called “illusionism,” though he regards that label as misleading and also calls it a “self-model approach.” This places him near Dennett, Frankish, Churchland-style neurophilosophical deflation, and higher-order representationalism, but with a distinctive focus: consciousness is not just a higher-order thought about a first-order percept; it is a schematic model of attention and its relation to self and world.

His relation to other scientific theories is also revealing. Global Workspace Theory explains consciousness partly through widespread access or broadcasting of information; Integrated Information Theory begins from claimed essential properties of phenomenal experience and derives requirements for a physical substrate. Graziano’s view is closer to Global Workspace and higher-order theories than to IIT, but he thinks they need an additional layer: a model that explains why globally available or higher-order information is represented as a subjective experience. In his 2017 AI paper, he says Global Workspace Theory is, in effect, like AST with the attention-schema component removed. In a 2020 “standard model” paper, he and colleagues argue that attention schema, global workspace, higher-order thought, and illusionist theories may be reconcilable as parts of a deeper common framework.

3. The core theory: attention, schema, awareness

Graziano begins with attention, not with qualia. Attention is a selective process by which some signals are enhanced, prioritized, stabilized, or deeply processed at the expense of others. It can be driven bottom-up by salient stimuli or top-down by goals, tasks, and expectations. The crucial claim is that sophisticated top-down control of attention requires an internal model of attention’s current state, dynamics, and consequences. This is the “attention schema.” The analogy is the body schema: the brain does not merely have a body; it constructs a simplified internal model of the body to control movement, predict posture, and interpret others’ bodies. Similarly, it does not merely attend; it constructs a simplified internal model of attention to control attention, predict attention, and interpret attention in others.

The most important distinction is this: awareness is not identical to attention. Attention is the underlying selective processing. Awareness is what results when the brain models that selective processing and makes the model available, at least partially, to cognition, report, planning, and social attribution. The 2015 formulation says that the brain constructs a schematic model of attention “in much the same way” it constructs a body schema, and that the content of this model leads the brain to conclude that it has subjective experience. It also predicts dissociations: attention can occur without awareness, but when awareness is absent, control of attention should be impaired.

A useful way to formalize Graziano’s view is: consciousness involves a relation among self-model, content-model, and attention-model. Suppose the brain represents an apple. A merely perceptual system might encode color, shape, location, and affordances. A system with an attention schema also represents that the self has a kind of mental possession of the apple. The report “I am aware of the apple” is therefore not the direct voice of an immaterial witness; it is the output of an information-processing system whose self-model contains a schematic relation of attention to content. In the 2017 article, Graziano says the attention schema describes the act of focusing resources on something—what attention is, what it does, its stable properties, its dynamics, and its consequences—and this information enables the machine to say it has a “mental grasp” of the apple.

This explains why consciousness seems mysterious. The model is not a neuroscientific description of neurons, gain modulation, recurrent processing, synaptic weights, or control signals. It is a coarse, practical, low-detail model. Therefore, when higher cognition queries the model, it does not find physical machinery. It finds a schematic property: mental possession, awareness, vividness, “experienceness.” Graziano’s 2022 paper states that AST represents attention inaccurately as a ghostly, nonphysical mental essence or vividness; it is a shell model or caricature of attention’s surface properties, not its mechanistic details.

This is also why introspective certainty is suspect. Graziano’s first principle is that information coming out of a brain must have been in that brain. If a person believes, insists, writes, and argues that they have a subjective feeling, that belief and those reports must be caused by information represented somewhere in the system. His second principle is that brain models are never perfectly accurate; they are adaptive simplifications. The color white, a phantom limb, or bodily self-location may be experientially compelling without being physically literal. Applied to consciousness, this means that the feeling of an ineffable inner property is not automatically veridical just because the system reports it with certainty.

This gives Graziano’s response to the hard problem. He does not begin by asking how matter produces a private, intangible essence. He asks: what real physical process is being modeled in an imperfect way such that the model leads us to believe and claim that we have an intangible essence? In AST, the real process is attention; the imperfect model is the attention schema; the resulting belief is consciousness-as-experienceness. In his 2022 paper, he calls this a “2 + 2” framework: once one accepts that brain outputs require brain information and that brain models are imperfect, the alleged mystical entity is replaced by a tractable problem of neuroscience and evolution.

4. What AST can and cannot explain

Graziano is careful, at least in his more systematic work, not to claim that AST explains every mental phenomenon. It explains why some mental contents are accompanied by a belief or report of subjective experience. It does not explain memory as such, emotion as such, decision-making as such, or creativity as such. Rather, it explains why, after a memory, emotion, decision, or creative act occurs, the system may represent that process as something it consciously experienced. In the 2022 PNAS article, he says AST can explain expert control of attention, attribution of minds to others, beliefs and claims about subjective experience, evolutionary distribution, and a route toward artificial consciousness; but it cannot explain most mental processes, creativity, or the emergence of an actual intangible essence, soul, or phenomenal ghost from brain activity.

That limitation is philosophically crucial. Graziano is not offering a full theory of all cognition. He is offering a theory of the experiential framing of cognition: why some percepts, thoughts, emotions, and decisions are represented as belonging to a subject in the mode of awareness. Put differently, AST is not a theory of the entire “bucket” of mental contents; it is a theory of why the bucket seems to have the special property of “experienceness.” Graziano explicitly distinguishes consciousness as experience-ness from the many items that can be experienced, such as perceptions, decisions, memories, and emotions.

5. Neuroscientific program and evidence

The empirical program around AST has several layers: behavioral dissociations between attention and awareness, neural networks involved in awareness and social cognition, clinical syndromes such as neglect, and artificial neural-network models. The theory predicts that attention and awareness should usually covary, because awareness is a model of attention. But they should sometimes dissociate, because models are imperfect. More specifically, attention without awareness should be possible, especially exogenous or stimulus-driven attention, but endogenous control of attention should degrade when the system lacks awareness of the attended item. Graziano’s 2022 review says this pattern is supported by experiments showing that without consciousness of an item, attention to the item may still occur, but control of attention with respect to that item breaks down.

One behavioral test is the 2020 Progress in Neurobiology paper on attention control and AST. Its reported highlights state that awareness of task rules was not necessary for exogenous or endogenous attention, but awareness of the stimulus was necessary for endogenous attention while not being necessary for exogenous attention; the paper says each result was specifically predicted by AST. This supports the distinction between being pulled by a stimulus and being able to strategically control attention toward or away from it.

A computational test appears in the 2021 PNAS paper on an AST neural-network agent. There, Wilterson and Graziano trained an artificial deep Q-learning agent to control visuospatial attention. The key result, summarized by PNAS and related abstracts, is that an artificial neural network could control visual attention when given an attention schema, while performance was greatly reduced without it. This does not prove human consciousness is an attention schema, but it supports the control-theoretic premise that an internal model of attention can have practical value for controlling attention.

The neural evidence focuses heavily, though not exclusively, on networks around the temporoparietal junction or TPJ, especially the right TPJ. Graziano links TPJ both to social cognition and to awareness/attention disorders. The 2022 paper notes that damage to the right TPJ is associated with hemispatial neglect, in which patients may process stimuli on one side unconsciously while lacking conscious experience and endogenous attentional control for that side. Graziano interprets the converging evidence cautiously: a cortical network with some emphasis on TPJ may be involved in modeling others’ attention, modeling one’s own attention, and controlling attention; when damaged, reported conscious experience can be profoundly disrupted.

A 2014 PNAS study, Attributing awareness to oneself and to others, tested the relationship between reported visual awareness and social attribution of awareness. Its significance statement says a region of human cortex was active when people attributed sensory awareness to someone else and that disrupting that region disrupted the person’s own sensory awareness, suggesting a fundamental link between private awareness and social cognition. A 2016 PNAS study on visual awareness independent of visual attention reported cortical networks involved in visual awareness and emphasized the importance of separating awareness from attention when testing for neural substrates. A 2021 PNAS study on attention, awareness, and the right TPJ predicted and found right TPJ involvement in the interaction by which awareness helps control attention.

The “social brain” side of the evidence is equally central. AST says an attention schema is useful not only for modeling one’s own attention but also for modeling the attention of others, which improves behavioral prediction. This makes consciousness-attribution partly continuous with theory of mind. Graziano’s group has studied how people implicitly represent others’ gaze and attention, including the striking 2019 PNAS finding that people automatically and unconsciously treat others’ eyes as if invisible, force-carrying beams project from them. A 2020 PNAS fMRI study reported that other people’s gaze is encoded as implied motion in the human brain, and a 2023 PNAS study reported that the human brain constructs a predictive model of others’ attention that is more involved than moment-by-moment monitoring of gaze direction. Recent work has continued this line, including studies on monitoring attention in self and others.

The empirical status should be stated carefully. These findings are not decisive proof of AST. TPJ is multifunctional, attention and awareness are experimentally difficult to separate, and different consciousness theories can sometimes reinterpret the same evidence. But AST is stronger than a mere philosophical slogan because it generates testable claims: attention without awareness should be possible but poorly controllable; self-awareness and other-awareness should recruit overlapping modeling machinery; neural or computational systems with an attention schema should control attention better than systems without one; and social cognition should benefit from modeling attention as a simplified, sometimes physically incoherent construct.

6. Evolutionary view

Graziano’s evolutionary story is gradualist and functional. Attention, in its simplest form, may have begun with early nervous systems as competition among signals. More complex forms of attention appear with vertebrate sensorimotor systems and forebrain control. An attention schema, if useful for controlling attention, would plausibly evolve wherever sufficiently complex endogenous attention exists. Graziano speculates that some components may be present in mammals, birds, nonavian reptiles, and perhaps independently in octopuses, while human language and culture add the capacity to form explicit philosophical and scientific doctrines about “consciousness.”

This is a significant philosophical move. Consciousness is not a metaphysical light that suddenly switches on at one biological boundary. It is an architecture-dependent, graded capacity involving attention, control models, self-modeling, other-modeling, and explicit report. Human consciousness is distinctive not because humans alone possess a magical property, but because human attention schemas are embedded in rich linguistic, cultural, autobiographical, and social cognition systems.

7. Artificial consciousness

Artificial consciousness is not incidental to Graziano; he explicitly discusses it. In the 2017 Frontiers in Robotics and AI paper, he says AST is offered as a possible starting point for building artificial consciousness. Given current technology, he argues, it should be possible to build a machine with a rich internal model of what consciousness is, able to attribute consciousness to itself and to people it interacts with, and able to use those attributions to predict human behavior. He says such a machine would “believe” and act as if it is conscious in the same sense that the human machine believes and acts as if it is conscious.

The philosophical implication is functionalist and multiple-realizability-friendly. If consciousness is the right kind of self-model of attention embedded in the right cognitive and social-control architecture, then it is not tied in principle to carbon, neurons, or biological tissue. A machine could instantiate the relevant structure. But this is not the same as saying that any language model that says “I am conscious” is conscious. On Graziano’s terms, the relevant system would need more than verbal output: it would need an internal attention model with functional roles in controlling its own processing and modeling other agents. The 2021 neural-network work gives a narrow computational proof of concept for attention-control benefits, not a full conscious machine.

Graziano also gives an ethical and safety-oriented argument: machines that can model consciousness in themselves and others may be better social agents, whereas systems that fail to attribute conscious states to humans risk being socially “sociopathic.” In the 2017 article, he explicitly frames machine consciousness as potentially necessary for future AI that treats people with humanistic care. This is one of his more speculative claims, but it follows naturally from the social-brain side of AST: to interact safely with agents, an intelligent system must model their attention, awareness, and mental states.

8. The philosophy of mind extracted from Graziano

The comprehensive philosophy of mind implicit in Graziano can be organized around several theses.

First, ontological physicalism. There is no nonphysical mind-stuff, phenomenal essence, soul, or intrinsic mental glow over and above physical information-processing systems. Graziano’s view does not deny that brains have real processes corresponding to what we call conscious life. It denies that introspection accurately reveals those processes as a private, nonphysical property. The real ontology consists of brains, bodies, environments, information, models, attention systems, control systems, and social-cognitive systems.

Second, representational functionalism. Mental states are understood by their roles in the system: what they model, what they control, what they predict, and what actions or reports they enable. Consciousness is not a substance but a functional-representational structure. In philosophy-of-mind terms, Graziano is close to functionalism, but not generic functionalism: he identifies a specific function, namely the modeling and control of attention, plus the social attribution of attention-like mental states.

Third, self-model theory. The subject of experience is not a Cartesian ego directly acquainted with qualia. It is a constructed self-model that includes information about body, attention, agency, and mental possession. Consciousness arises when the system models a relation between self and content: “this item is in my awareness.” The self is not fictitious in the sense that nothing exists; it is model-dependent in the sense that the system’s self-knowledge is partial, schematic, and pragmatically designed.

Fourth, illusionism in a moderate or model-theoretic sense. The illusion is not that nothing real is happening. Attention is real. Neural processing is real. Self-modeling is real. The illusion is that the model’s content is interpreted as revealing an extra, nonphysical essence. Graziano’s 2024 work broadens illusionism into a family of options and argues that because internal models are never fully accurate, some illusionist layer is probably necessary for any workable theory of consciousness or introspection.

Fifth, anti-incorrigibilism about introspection. Graziano rejects the idea that introspection is a transparent window into the essence of mind. Introspection is itself a brain process accessing internal models. When we say “I know consciousness exists because I feel it,” we are not stepping outside the system; we are reporting the system’s own model. This does not make introspection useless, but it makes it epistemically fallible and theoretically indirect.

Sixth, social externalization of mind-attribution. Consciousness is not only self-awareness; it is also the attribution of awareness to others. The same or overlapping machinery that models one’s own attention can model another person’s attention. This makes “mind” a social-cognitive construct as well as an introspective one. The philosophical problem of other minds is reframed: we do not infer a hidden Cartesian theater in others; we apply an attention schema to predict behavior, interpret gaze, and attribute awareness.

Seventh, mental causation without epiphenomenal qualia. On Graziano’s view, consciousness has causal roles because the attention schema has causal roles. It helps control attention, structure cognition, guide reports, and model others. What is causally active is not an ineffable quale but the representational machinery. This allows him to avoid both dualist interaction problems and epiphenomenalism: consciousness, properly reinterpreted as a model, does work.

Eighth, graded animal and machine consciousness. Because consciousness is an architecture and not a metaphysical essence, it comes in degrees and forms. Animals with sophisticated attention control may possess partial attention-schema machinery; humans add linguistic and cultural elaboration; machines could, in principle, instantiate analogous structures if they build and use internal models of attention and awareness.

9. Strengths of Graziano’s position

The first strength is explanatory economy. AST explains why consciousness seems nonphysical without positing anything nonphysical. It turns the hard problem into a problem about self-modeling, attention, control, and social cognition. It thereby explains not only reports of experience but also the peculiar metaphysical character of those reports: ineffability, privacy, certainty, and the feeling that awareness is “inside” us.

The second strength is mechanistic specificity. Many philosophical theories say consciousness is representation, higher-order thought, access, integration, or self-modeling. Graziano specifies what is modeled: attention. He also specifies why modeling it matters: model-based control and social prediction. This gives AST empirical bite that many purely philosophical accounts lack.

The third strength is its bridge between first-person and third-person discourse. AST explains why first-person consciousness feels obvious while remaining scientifically indirect. It neither dismisses conscious reports as meaningless nor treats them as metaphysical data. It treats them as data about internal models. This is close to Dennettian heterophenomenology, but with a proposed neural and computational mechanism.

The fourth strength is integration. Graziano can reinterpret Global Workspace Theory as describing the availability or broadcasting of contents, Higher-Order Theory as describing metarepresentational structure, and AST as explaining why such contents are represented as subjectively experienced. His “standard model” ambition is to show that major theories may be addressing different layers of the same architecture rather than wholly competing mechanisms.

10. Main objections and unresolved issues

The deepest objection is that AST may solve the meta-problem rather than the hard problem. It explains why we claim, believe, and are certain that we have consciousness. But a phenomenal realist will say that explaining consciousness reports does not explain why there is something it is like to see red, feel pain, or think. Chalmers explicitly distinguishes the meta-problem from the hard problem, and says that although solving the meta-problem may illuminate consciousness, it does not automatically eliminate the need to explain phenomenal experience itself. Graziano’s reply is that the supposed further explanandum is a misleading construction of the attention schema; the critic’s reply is that this changes the subject.

A second objection is the “illusion requires experience” worry. If consciousness is an illusion, what is it like to undergo the illusion? Graziano’s best answer is that “illusion” should not mean a conscious show occurring in a hidden theater. It means a misrepresentation inside an information-processing system, as in perceptual illusions or body-schema distortions. But critics may argue that perceptual illusion analogies already presuppose phenomenality, and therefore cannot explain phenomenality from scratch. This is where Graziano’s moderate, model-based illusionism is most vulnerable to philosophers who take first-person experience as epistemically basic.

A third issue is neural specificity. TPJ, ventral attention networks, social cognition networks, and neglect syndromes are suggestive, but no single region is “the consciousness organ.” TPJ participates in many functions: attention reorienting, social cognition, body representation, self–other distinction, and more. Graziano recognizes that the evidence points to a network with TPJ emphasis, not a simple localization. The theory’s future depends on increasingly precise dissociations among attention, awareness, report, social attribution, and control.

A fourth issue concerns scope. AST explains the sense of awareness attached to contents; it does not explain the full richness of perception, emotion, memory, selfhood, or agency. Graziano openly admits this. A complete philosophy of mind built from AST therefore requires supplementary theories of perception, affect, memory, embodiment, language, and action. AST is a theory of “experienceness,” not a complete cognitive architecture.

A fifth issue is artificial consciousness. If a machine can model attention and claim consciousness, does that suffice for moral patienthood? Graziano’s functionalist answer pushes toward yes, if the architecture is genuinely analogous. But critics may worry that systems can mimic self-attribution without having the right internal control structure, or that the relevant structure may be too thin to ground ethical status. Graziano’s theory gives a design criterion for artificial consciousness, but it does not by itself settle the ethics of artificial suffering, rights, or personhood.

Conclusion

Michael Graziano’s position is one of the most developed neuroscientific forms of contemporary illusionist physicalism. Historically, it stands after behaviorism, identity theory, functionalism, higher-order thought, global workspace theory, Dennettian heterophenomenology, and Chalmers’s hard-problem challenge. Its distinctive move is to identify the relevant self-model as an attention schema: a simplified, useful, inaccurate model of the brain’s own selective processing, extended to the modeling of other minds.

The philosophy of mind one can extract from Graziano is not that consciousness is “nothing.” It is that consciousness, as ordinarily conceived, is a misdescription of something real. The real thing is a complex architecture of attention, self-modeling, body-modeling, social cognition, and report. The misleading thing is the introspective interpretation of that architecture as a private, nonphysical essence. If Graziano is right, the traditional hard problem is not solved by adding a new metaphysical ingredient; it is dissolved by explaining why brains model themselves in a way that makes such an ingredient seem unavoidable.
